coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of N,S-doped carbon quantum dots as a dual mode sensor for azo dye tartrazine and fluorescent ink applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: John, Bony K.
Mathew, Sneha
John, Neenamol
Mathew, Jincy
Mathew, Beena
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, blue-emitting N,S-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) were fabricated via a hydrothermal process from a mixture of o-phenylenediamine and methionine. They exhibit photoluminous (PL) emission at 440 nm, with a quantum yield (QY) of 20 %. Various physio-chemical approaches were employed to explore the structural, morphological, optical and electrochemical behaviour of the N,S-CQDs. The functional groups such as carboxyl (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH), amino (–NH2) and thiol (-SH) on the N,S-CQDs surface allows it to have good QY, and excellent water solubility. The proposed sensor could detect tartrazine (TT), a common synthetic food dye using fluorescence and electrochemical approaches. The fluorescence method achieved a limit of detection (LOD) of 80 nM within the linear range of 0–10 μM. A combination of static quenching process and inner filter effect (IFE) was involved in the sensing mechanism. In electrochemical sensing, the differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) method was successfully applied within the linear range of 5–35 nM and a LOD of 2 nM. Both methods were successfully used to determine tartrazine levels in soft drinks and candies. In addition, the N,S-CQDs based fluorescent ink was developed for writing, drawing and fingerprint imaging applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Doping
Tartrazine
Fluorescence
Quantum yield
Electrochemical

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optimizations of the sensing of tartrazine
Interference, Reproducibility, Stability, and impedance analysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors were grateful for the financial assistance from UGC India for the Senior Research Fellowship (Grant number BININ01764209).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical behaviour of N,S-CQDs; (a) UV–vis. spectra of N,S-CQDs, the colour of N,S-CQD solution under daylight and on UV irradiation are given in the inset, (b) fluorescence emission spectra of N,S-CQDs over excitation wavelengths from 310 to 390 nm.
(a)TEM, (b) HR-TEM, (c) SAED, and (d) particle size distribution diagram of N,S-CQDs.
(a) EDX, (b) FT-IR, (c) XRD pattern, and (d) Raman spectrum of N,S-CQDs.
XPS of N,S-CQDs; (a) survey spectrum, and deconvoluted XPS spectra of (b) C1s, (c) N 1 s, (d) S 2p, and (e) O 1 s.

Fluorescence behaviour of N,S-CQDs (a) on interacting with various dye molecules, (b) on increasing the concentration of TT molecules from 0 to 15 μM, (c) fluorescence response (F0/F) plot of a mixture of N,S-CQDs and TT molecules while increasing the concentration of TT from 0 to 10 μM, (d) zeta potential diagram of N,S-CQDs, (e) zeta potential diagram of N,S-CQD-TT mixture, and (f) photograph of N,S-CQD solutions contain 10 μM of various dye molecules upon UV light irradiation.
(a) PL response (F/F0) of N,S-CQDs-TT mixture contains 50 μM interfering dye molecules, and (b) time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of N,S-CQDs in the presence and absence of TT molecules.

(a) CV curve of bare PtE, PtE/N,S-CQD, and PtE/N,S-CQD/TT in 1 mmol/L K3Fe(CN)6, (b) optimization of pH, (c) scan rate study, (d) plot of the square root of scan rate vs peak current, (e) DPV curves of PtE/N,S-CQD in presence of TT molecules having varying concentrations from 5 to 35 nM at pH 7, and (f) TT concentration vs peak current plot.
(a) Chronoamperometric diagram, (b) repeatability, (c) stability, and (d) PEIS spectra of PtE/N,S-CQDs.
Photographs of (a) hand-written words and hand-drawn images and (b) fingerprint imaging using N,S-CQDs as ink on filter paper under 360 nm UV light irradiation.
Schematic representation of the various steps involved in the preparation of N,S-CQDs.
Probable electron transfer mechanism for the detection of TT molecules by the PtE/N,S-CQDs.
Fluorescent detection of TT molecules in food samples (n = 3).
Electrochemical detection of TT molecules in food samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of N,S-doped carbon quantum dots as a dual mode sensor for azo dye tartrazine and fluorescent ink applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686 560, Kerala, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Sciences, Mahatma Gandhi University, Kottayam 686 560, Kerala, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, blue-emitting N,S-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CQDs) were fabricated via a hydrothermal process from a mixture of o-phenylenediamine and methionine. They exhibit photoluminous (PL) emission at 440 nm, with a quantum yield (QY) of 20 %. Various physio-chemical approaches were employed to explore the structural, morphological, optical and electrochemical behaviour of the N,S-CQDs. The functional groups such as carboxyl (–COOH), hydroxyl (–OH), amino (–NH2) and thiol (-SH) on the N,S-CQDs surface allows it to have good QY, and excellent water solubility. The proposed sensor could detect tartrazine (TT), a common synthetic food dye using fluorescence and electrochemical approaches. The fluorescence method achieved a limit of detection (LOD) of 80 nM within the linear range of 0–10 μM. A combination of static quenching process and inner filter effect (IFE) was involved in the sensing mechanism. In electrochemical sensing, the differential pulse voltammetric (DPV) method was successfully applied within the linear range of 5–35 nM and a LOD of 2 nM. Both methods were successfully used to determine tartrazine levels in soft drinks and candies. In addition, the N,S-CQDs based fluorescent ink was developed for writing, drawing and fingerprint imaging applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new family of fluorescent nanomaterials invented in 2004 by Xu et al. [1]. The CQDs typically exhibited a size of less than 10 nm and were found in discrete, quasi-spherical, nanocrystalline, or amorphous carbon structural shapes [2]. Recently, they have received much attention because of their excellent water solubility, good photostability, low toxicity, and promising biocompatibility [3,4]. Several reported applications of CQDs include fluorescent sensing and biosensing, bioimaging, electrochemical sensing, food addictive detection, nanodevices, cellular imaging, antimicrobial agent, and biomedicine [5–9]. Various reported methods for CQD synthesis include arc discharge [10], laser ablation [11], hydrothermal treatment [12], electrochemical oxidation [13] and microwave irradiation [14]. Out of these, fabrication through the hydrothermal method has exhibited several advantages over other methods since this method provides simple procedures, facile routes, eco-friendly and high quantum yield (QY). Recently, the successful synthesis of CQDs from chitosan, R. graveolens leaves, acrylamide, and grass has been reported [15,16]. These were designed as probes for recognizing various metal ions, pesticides, biomolecules etc. [17,18].

Different synthetic colours are commonly used in many foods worldwide, including drinks, jelly, fruit juice, candies, sweets, and other foods. Despite their numerous applications, the harmful effects of these synthetic dyes cannot be overlooked. Tartrazine (TT) is a common synthetic food colourant found in various foods, including candy, beverages, bakery items, and dairy goods [19,20]. However, several investigations have found that excessive TT consumption might result in adverse health effects such as alterations in hepatic and renal parameters, reproductive toxicity, hyperactivity, asthma, allergies, frequent headache and neurobehavioral poisoning [21–24]. As a result, their inclusion in food should be strictly regulated by rules and regulations, and there is a necessity to develop an effective testing technology to determine TT in foods in terms of rapidity, simplicity, and sensitivity.

Until now, several analytical techniques for analyzing tartrazine in food products, such as thin-layer chromatography (TLC) [25] LC-MS method [26] spectrophotometry,[27] and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), have been increasingly used [28]. However, these techniques are costly, time-consuming, require specialized equipment and complicated sample preparation, limiting frequent monitoring of TT in food samples.

Fluorescence spectroscopy has been a popular method of chemical analysis for many years due to its flexibility, sensitivity, rapidity, and non-destructive nature. Significant efforts have been made in recent years to investigate efficient fluorescent probes for detecting different metal ions, chemical substances, and biomolecules [29,30]. Electrochemical sensors could provide a simple, low-cost, sensitive, and rapid sensing platform for analyzing electroactive analytes [31,32]. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), differential pulse voltammetry (DPV), and amperometric analysis are the important electroanalytical options to monitor the analytes in qualitative and quantitative forms in a food sample.

In this paper, we suggested a simple, low-cost, and environmentally friendly approach for producing luminous nitrogen and sulphur co-doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs), by hydrothermal treatment of a mixture of o-phenylenediamine and methionine. Based on the extent of quenching by the various analyte molecules, the obtained blue fluorescent N,S-CQDs have been designed as a PL sensor for the detection of tartrazine. Furthermore, the platinum electrode surface was modified for the sensitive trace measurement of tartrazine by drop-casting the N,S-CQD suspension on the electrode (Pt-N,S-CQDs). Coupling the N,S-CQDs with the Pt electrode results in enhanced surface area and high electrical conductivity. Both PL and electrochemical methods exhibited excellent analytical performance in determining TT in food samples. To the best of our knowledge, N,S-CQD modified Pt electrodes for tartrazine detection have not yet been reported. In addition, the fabricated N,S-CQD solution was used as fluorescent ink for writing, drawing and fingerprint imaging applications.

o-Phenylenediamine (C₆H₄(NH₂)₂), methionine (C5H11NO2S), quinine sulfate (99.0 %), CdCl2·H2O, sulphuric acid (H2SO4), alizarin red S (ARS), acid fuchsin (AF), methylene blue (MB), malachite green (MG), rhodamine B (Rh B), sunset yellow (SY), tartrazine (TT), xylene orange (XO), allura red (AR), congo red (CR), and methyl orange (MO) were obtained from Merck, India.

The absorption spectra were recorded in the 200–800 nm range using a Shimadzu UV-3600 Plus UV–vis. spectrometer. A Shimadzu RF-6000 spectrofluorometer was used to study the fluorescence behaviour and quenching effect. Vibrational spectroscopic analysis was performed on a Perkin-Elmer spectrometer in the 400–4000 cm−1 range. Bruker AXS D8 Advance Xray Diffractometer was employed to collect X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS). The morphological and structural information from the transmission electron microscopic (TEM) studies were executed on a JEOL-JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope. The particle size distribution and zeta potential examination were made on a Horiba SZ-100 nanoparticle analyzer. A Biologic SP-200 electrochemical workstation was employed to execute the electrochemical investigations.

The N,S-CQDs were prepared from the hydrothermal treatment of a mixture of o-phenylenediamine and methionine in distilled water. In a typical procedure, about 0.7 g o-phenylenediamine and 0.4 g methionine were added to 60 mL of distilled water. The mixture was transferred into a 200 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and was heated at 210 °C for 5 h. After heating, the autoclave was allowed to cool in a fume hood naturally. The resulting brownish-yellow solution was centrifuged at 2000 rpm, followed by filtration using a 0.22 μm membrane. The freeze-drying of the solution yielded powdered N,S-CQDs, which were further dissolved in water to obtain 0.30 mg/mL N,S-CQD solution. The final N,S-CQD aqueous solution was stored at 4 °C for characterization and further use. Various steps involved in the synthetic procedure is illustrated in Scheme 1 .

The quantum yield (QY) of the developed N,S-CQDs were evaluated by taking quinine sulphate in 0.1 M H2SO4, having a QY of 54 % as the standard. The comparative method was used for the QY experiments at 360 nm excitation based on the equation,
(1)
Φ S
=
Φ R
×
μ s
μ R
×
η s
η R
2
where ΦS and ΦR represent the QY of the sample and standard, respectively. The slopes of the plot of integrated fluorescence intensity versus the absorbance plot of the sample and reference are indicated by μS and μR, respectively. The symbol ηs and ηR denotes the refractive indices of the sample and reference solutions.

The detection of TT molecules using the fluorescence method was achieved in a phosphate buffer solution (PBS) of pH 7 with an interaction period of 4 min. N,S-CQD solution having 0.30 mg/mL was used in the complete analysis. Different concentrations of TT solutions were made and added to the mixture containing N,S-CQD solution and PBS with a final volume of 3 mL. The interaction between TT molecules and the N,S-CQDs resulted in the PLquenching effect at room temperature. The sensitivity investigations were performed by applying 0–10 μM concentrations of TT molecules and plotting the corresponding change in PL intensity. The selectivity of N,S-CQD for TT molecules over other analyte molecules was examined by introducing 30 μM interfering molecules to the N,S-CQD solution containing 10 μM TT molecules and plotting the associated changes in PL intensity after 4 min interaction period.

A platinum electrode (PtE) was selected as the working electrode for the electrochemical investigations, along with a platinum wire as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. The electrochemical experiments were performed using N,S-CQD drop-casted Pt electrode (PtE/N,S-CQD) at pH 7. Cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) studies were carried out using TT solutions in the 5–35 nM concentration range.

The N,S-CQD solutions were injected directly into a pen without any chemical treatment and used as fluorescent ink. Under UV light at a wavelength of 360 nm, the words, images, and fingerprints on the filter paper could be clearly observed.

The optical features of the developed N,S-CQDs was examined through UV–vis. absorption and fluorescence emission spectral analyses [33]. The UV–vis. analysis of fabricated N,S-CQDs (Fig. 1 a) exhibited two absorption bands at 265 and 318 nm from the π–π* transition aromatic sp2 groups bonds and the n-π* transitions in –COOH, or –NH2 groups, respectively. The PL spectrum of N,S-CQDs have demonstrated that the optimal emission peak was positioned at 440 nm when excited at 360 nm (Fig. 1a). The inset photograph in Fig. 1a indicates the brown N,S-CQD aqueous solution under visible and blue emitting N,S-CQD solution under UV illumination at 360 nm. To investigate the optical properties further, the PL emission spectra of the N,S-CQDs was collected at progressively increasing excitation wavelengths, as shown in Fig. 1b. The emission spectra of N,S-CQDs show a red shift from 310 to 390 nm with increasing excitation wavelengths, with a maximum PL emission at 440 nm on 360 nm excitation. This finding confirmed that the excitation wavelength significantly affects the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs [34]. The obtained QY of the system using quinine sulphate as the standard was about 20 %. The plot for quantum yield calculation is given in Fig. S1. The N and S doping and availability of electron-rich auxochromic functional groups are mainly responsible for this relatively high QY value.

TEM study was executed to determine the size and morphology of the fabricated N,S-CQDs. The TEM image shown in Fig. 2 a demonstrates that N,S-CQDs are spherical and well dispersed without any aggregation. The presence of some kind of crystallinity inside the individual N,S-CQDs were evident from the HR-TEM analysis (Fig. 2b) with a fringe width of 0.26 nm, similar to the lattice spacing in graphitic carbon atoms [35]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern shown in Fig. 2c demonstrates the amorphous nature of the system as a whole. The particle size distribution plot in Fig. 2d showed that the spherical N,S-CQDs have small size distribution ranging from 2 to 6 nm, with an average size of 3.8 nm. Additionally, using the dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique, the average particle size was determined to be 4.75 nm, which is very close to the particle size obtained by TEM experiments (Fig. S2). The size of the synthesized quantum dots was further examined by employing atomic force microscopy (AFM). The results (Fig. S3 a and S3b) suggest that the particle size is substantially below 10 nm, which is comparable with the results in TEM and DLS analysis.

The EDX result in Fig. 3a demonstrates the availability of elements such as carbon (C), nitrogen (N), sulphur (S), and oxygen (O) in the synthesized N,S-CQDs system, with C 54.33 wt%, O 28.80 wt%, N 10.60 wt% and S 6.27 wt%. To investigate the functional groups present on the N,S-CQD surface, FT-IR analysis was carried out. As displayed in Fig. 3b characteristic vibrational bands of the –NH and –OH stretching modes were observed at about 3289 and 3080 cm−1, respectively. The band at 2490 cm−1 was attributed to the S H stretching mode. The peaks obtained at 1724 and 1635 cm−1 were due to the CO and CC/CN stretching vibrations. Another band at 1422 cm−1 can be assigned to the CN stretching vibrations. A band at 1140 cm−1 indicates the symmetric stretching vibrations of the CS bond, and the band corresponding to the COC stretching was found at 1030 cm−1 [36]. The vibrational band for CO stretching occurred at 880 cm−1. These findings confirm that the excellent hydrophilicity, stability, and fluorescence emission behaviour of N,S-CQDs are originated from the presence of different surface functional groups such as –COOH, –OH, -SH and –NH2 [37].

In Fig. 3c, the XRD pattern of N,S-CQDs displayed a broad peak centred at 2θ = 25.16°, which corresponds to the specified (0 0 2) peak (JCPDS No. 26–1076) [38,39]. This observation suggests that the N,S-CQDs exhibit an amorphous structure [40]. The Raman spectrum of the N,S-CQDs given in Fig. 3d reveals two distinct peaks at 1334 (D band) and 1530 cm−1 (G-Band) due to the attached carbonyl groups to the N,S-CQD surface and a typical carbon nanostructure core vibrations, respectively [41]. The ID/IG ratio was 0.84; which confirms the presence of highly graphitized carbon atoms in the as-synthesized N,S-CQDs [42].

The surface states and chemical composition of fabricated N,S-CQDs were studied using the XPS spectral analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, the XPS survey spectrum shows four characteristic peaks at 139.4, 288.5, 401.5 and 531.5 eV, corresponding to S2p, C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. The composition of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and sulphur in the sample was about 56.6, 28.4, 9.7 and 5.3 %, respectively. High-resolution XPS spectra of the C1s spectrum display-three peaks at 284.5, 285.4, and 288.8.3 eV, which are attributed to the CC, CN/CS and CO bonds, respectively (Fig. 4b). The N1s deconvoluted XPS spectra in Fig. 4c revealed two binding energy peaks at 399.3 eV due to the CNC group and 400.4 eV, associated with NC/NH groups present on the N,S-CQD surface [43]. The high-resolution S2p (Fig. 4d) exhibited two characteristic peaks at 162.4 and 165.6 eV, corresponding to the S2p3/2 and S2p1/2, respectively. Deconvoluted O1s spectra in Fig. 4e unveiled three prominent binding energy peaks at 531.4, 532.5 and 533.7 eV confirming the availability of CO, C–OH, and OC-O bonds, respectively [44]. Both XPS and FT-IR results significantly match and ensure the availability of the functional moieties such as –COOH, –OH, –NH2 and -SH on the surface of N,S-CQDs.

The luminous property of the as-synthesized N,S-CQDs were investigated at various pH, ionic strength, visible light irradiation time, and storage time. The pH stability analysis in the pH range 3–12 (Fig. S4a) demonstrated that N,S-CQDs are very stable in the pH range 3–9, after that a gradual decrease in PL intensity was observed. The PL emission intensities of N,S-CQDs at different ionic strengths of KCl ranging from 0 to 3 M did not show a significant change in the emission intensity as given in Fig. S4b. In addition, continuous exposure to visible light for about 3 h had no profound effect on the fluorescence of N,S-CQDs. (Fig. S4c). Furthermore, the long-term storage stability of N,S-CQDs was investigated, and the fluorescence intensity of N,S-CQDs remained unaltered over 3 months of storage, as shown in Fig. S4d. This allows the storage of N,S-CQDs in a homogenous phase with no aggregation for a long time. These findings confirm that fabricated N,S-CQDs have exceptional stability to variable ionic strength, better tolerance to pH, outstanding photostability, and exceptionally long storage time.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimizations of the sensing of tartrazine
ce:section.ce:para: Experimental parameters, such as the pH of the solution and interaction time, were optimized to maximize the detection capacity of the sensor. The fluorescence spectra of N,S-CQD solution containing 10 μM TT molecules at different pH were measured, and a plot of relative fluorescence response (F0/F) of N-CQDs towards TT molecules at different pH values is illustrated in Fig. S5a. The N,S-CQDs showed a remarkable fluorescence quenching effect in the pH range of 4–9. The proposed sensor is an excellent option for various practical applications because the majority of water and biological samples have a pH within this range. The maximum quenching was observed at pH 7, and was selected as the optimal pH for further analysis. The protonation of the surface functional groups present on the N,S-CQDs in the acidic medium and the partial hydrolysis of the TT molecules in the basic medium reduces the interaction between N,S-CQDs and TT molecules, resulting in a decreased PL quenching effect at acidic and basic pH [45]. The time of interaction required for the maximum quenching of PL intensity by the TT molecules is displayed in Fig. S5b. It was found that complete interaction occurred within an interaction period of 4 min. and remained stable even after 10 min. These results indicate that the reaction between the functional groups on the N,S-CQD surface and TT molecules is quick and steady, so no strict time control is needed during the sensing process.

Photoluminescence studies were performed to study the selectivity of the proposed sensor towards TT by mixing 10 μM solutions with 30 μM various interfering analytes, including alizarin red S (ARS), acid fuchsin (AF), methylene blue (MB), malachite green (MG), rhodamine B (Rh B), sunset yellow (SY), tartrazine (TT), xylene orange (XO), allura red (AR), congo red (CR), and methyl orange (MO). From the plot of relative fluorescence response (F/F0) against equal quantities of various analytes in Fig. 5 a, a considerable quenching in PL intensity was observed only from the TT molecules, while no significant alteration in the PL intensity was appended from the other substances.

The Stern-Volmer equation and plot were used to describe the linear range and LOD of the system. The Stern-Volmer equation is given as,
(1)
F 0
/ F = 1 + K s v
Q
where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensities of N,S-CQDs in the absence and presence of TT ions at 440 nm. [Q] is the concentration of TT and Ksv represents the Stern-Volmer quenching constant. There was a gradual decrease in the PL intensities of N,S-CQDs as the concentration of TT increased from 0 to 15 μM, as illustrated in Fig. 5b. A plot of F0/F against TT concentration is demonstrated in Fig. 5c. From this plot, the standard deviation (σ) and the slope of the linear fit (m) was found to be 0.002 and 0.075 respectively. The LOD for the TT detection was assessed by the equation,
(2)
LOD = 3 σ / m

The LOD was found to be 80 nM with a linear range of 0–10 μM. A comparison of the LOD of this work with some of the early reported results is listed in Table S1. The zeta potential analysis of N,S-CQDs revealed (Fig. 5d) that the N,S-CQDs are negatively charged with a value of −34.6 mV owes to the several negatively charged functional groups on the N,S-CQD surface. However, the value becomes −1.09 mV after adding 10 μM TT into the N,S-CQD solution (Fig. 5e). The decrease in negative zeta potential value was due to the successful interaction between the N,S-CQDs and TT molecules. A photograph of N,S-CQD solutions comprising analytes molecules having 10 μM concentration under UV irradiation is shown in Fig. 5f, where only in the vial containing TT molecules a fluorescence quenching was observed.

To investigate the selectivity of this sensing system, the fluorescence response of the TT molecules towards N,S-CQDs at a concentration of 10 μM in the medium containing other foreign materials. Dyes such as alizarin red S (ARS), acid fuchsin (AF), methylene blue (MB), malachite green (MG), rhodamine B (Rh B), sunset yellow (SY), tartrazine (TT), xylene orange (XO), allura red (AR), congo red (CR), and methyl orange (MO) having 50 μM concentration were added to the N,S-CQD-TT mixture at a pH of 7 and an interaction time of 4 min. As depicted in Fig. 6 a, the interference from the interfering materials is negligible, and the proposed system could detect the TT ions sensitively and selectively.

Static and dynamic quenching are two possible phenomena that could result in fluorescence quenching. The UV–vis. absorption spectrum of N,S-CQDs, tartrazine, and N,S-CQD-tartrazine mixture is presented in Fig. S6. The TT molecule exhibited two broad absorption bands at 257 and 428 nm. In the absorption spectrum of N,S-CQD-TT mixture the absorbance of the peak at 256 nm decreases with a red shift. This indicates the formation of a ground state complex between TT and N,S-CQDs. Hence, the decrease in PL intensity was caused by the static quenching process. In addition, fluorescence lifetime analysis was also performed to confirm the quenching process. The sensing process involves static quenching; there is no change in the fluorescence lifetime of the sensor in the presence and absence of the quencher [46]. However, dynamic quenching involves a considerable difference in the fluorescence lifetime of the sensor in the presence of the quenching molecules. The fluorescence lifetimes analysis of N,S-CQD and N,S-CQD/TT system (Fig. 6b) revealed an average fluorescence lifetime 5.62 ns for N-CQDs with χ2 value of 1.01 in the absence of TT molecules, whereas that after the addition of TT molecules became 5.48 ns with χ2 value 1.03. Since there was almost no change in the PL lifetimes of N,S-CQDs before and after adding TT molecules, the possibility of a static quenching process is confirmed.

Generally, there are three possible mechanisms for the sensing process like fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), and inner filter effect (IFE). The absence of photoluminescence from TT molecules in the presence of UV light eliminates the possibility of FRET. The PL lifetimes of N-CQDs and N-CQDs-TT systems are almost similar; there is no prospect for a significant electron-transfer process between TT molecules and N-CQDs, which rules out the chance of PET. As evident from Fig. S6 there was considerable overlap between the absorption spectra of TT and the PL emission spectra of N,S-CQDs. Hence IFE mechanism might be responsible for the quenching mechanism. TT molecules behave as energy acceptors during the sensing process, whereas N,S-CQDs act as energy donors.

The electrochemical sensing ability of N,S-CQDs for TT was examined with the help of N,S-CQD modified platinum electrode (PtE/N,S-CQD) as the working electrode, a platinum wire as the counter electrode and an Ag/AgCl electrode as the reference electrode. Initially, CV analysis of bare PtE, PtE/N,S-CQDs and PtE/N,S-CQDs/TT electrodes were performed in 1 mmol/L K3Fe(CN)6 solution under the scan rate of 50 mVs−1. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a the brown-lined curve represents the CV curve of the bare Pt electrode. A pair of distinct redox peaks have appeared on the CV curve of PtE/N,S-CQDs indicated by the blue-lined curve. The redox peak current was further increased by adding 20 nM TT solution into the PtE/N,S-CQDs system, as represented by the red line curve. This may be due to the increase in the effective surface area of the electrode on N,S-CQD modification which resulted in the enhanced electron transfer process [47].

To improve the sensitivity of the detection method, the effect of pH on the electrochemical sensing of 20 nM TT solution on the PtE/N,S-CQD was evaluated using CV method. The CV curve obtained in the pH range from 3 to 7 using PBS is demonstrated in Fig. 7b. The highest peak current response was received at a pH of 7 and is selected as the optimum condition for further analysis. Scan rate studies were performed at pH 7 in 0.1 M PBS to investigate the sensing kinetics and mechanism. As shown in Fig. 7c the oxidation peak and reduction peak current currents of TT increased proportionally to the scan rate in the range 10–100 mVs−1. In Fig. 7d, a logarithmic plot of the peak current for TT versus the square root of the scan rate shows a linear correlation with R2 value of 0.99, demonstrating that diffusion controls the electrochemical process [48].

DPV technique was used to quantitatively analyze the TT present in the sample. The DPV response of TT having different concentrations under optimized conditions is shown in Fig. 7e. A linear relationship was observed between the current value and TT concentration. Fig. 7f shows the calibration curve plotted between the catalytic current and TT concentration, which established a linear relationship with the TT concentrations ranging from 5 − 35 nM with a R2 value of 0.995. From the standard deviation (σ) and slope (m) obtained from the calibration curve, the LOD of the system was calculated using the equation,
(3)
LOD = a + 3 σ / m

The determined LOD was about 2 nM with a wide linear range of 5–35 nM. A comparison between the system described herein and previously published electrochemical sensors are given in Table S2, confirming the excellent performance of the proposed detector in detecting TT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference, Reproducibility, Stability, and impedance analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Besides TT, other synthetic dyes like ARS, AF, MB, MG, Rh B, SY, TT, XO, AR, CR, IC and MO are also added to soft drinks, which may bring interferences to molecules. To study the potential interferences of these substances, the chronoamperometric technique was used by taking 100 nM interfering substances and 20 nM TT solutions at a pH of 7. The chronoamperometric diagram showing the current response at various time intervals is shown in Fig. 8 a. A specific current response was observed only when TT was added to the system, even in the presence of other analytes. The reproducibility of the analysis was validated by recording the CV response of the PtE/N,S-CQD for 20 nM TT solution for a continuous 70 cycles. As shown in Fig. 8b, there was no discernible difference in the area and position of the peaks after 70 cycles, indicating that the experimental results are repeatable. The stability of the developed N,S-CQDs was tested by storing the modified electrode in a desiccator for four weeks, and only a minor fluctuation in current response was noticed (Fig. 8c). Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was used to assess the resistance of the designed sensor (Fig. 8d). The resistivity of the modified N,S-CQD/PtE was lower than that of bare PtE. As a result, fast electron transfer and good electrical conductivity are possible in the modified system.

Tartrazine molecules undergo a redox reaction on the PtE/N,S-CQDs surface during the electrochemical reaction. The oxidation of TT occurs with the transfer of one proton and two electrons to the PtE/N,S-CQD surface. The hydroxyl group present on the imidazole ring in the TT gets oxidized to carbonyl during oxidation. The reduction process involves the absorption of one proton and two electrons from the electrode surface by the oxidized TT molecules [49,50]. A probable mechanism for the electrochemical detection of TT by the N,S-CQDs is presented in Scheme 2 .

We extended the designed approach for detecting TT in some food samples using PL and DPV analysis. Soft drinks and candy were selected for the study since they commonly contain this synthetic dye as a colourant. Table 1 and Table 2 show the recovery results for the food samples spiked with known amounts of standard TT. Both PL and DPV methods achieved good recoveries ranging from 96.7 to 104.25 %. These results show that the suggested approach is reliable and accurate for identifying tartrazine in food samples.

The developed aqueous N,S-CQD solution was effectively used as a fluorescent ink for writing letters, drawing patterns and collecting thumb impressions because of their exceptional solubility, stability, and excellent fluorescence property. An empty pen filled with the aqueous N,S-CQDs was used to write, draw and collect fingerprints easily on the filter paper and their images under 360 nm UV light irradiation, as shown in Fig. 9 a & b. The N,S-CQDS could be used to identify people from their fingerprints, which helps society to solve crimes and law enforcement. This reported fluorescent ink method for fingerprint analysis is a simple, rapid and eco-friendly technique compared to the widely used conventional powder dusting method [51]. Therefore, the suggested fluorescent ink is a suitable replacement for commonly used traditional fluorescent ink [52].

This work developed a novel fluorescent and electrochemical sensor based on N,S-CQDs to determine tartrazine in food samples directly. Characterization techniques including UV–vis., FT-IR, XPS, DLS, zeta potential, XRD, Raman, and TEM were employed to validate the successful synthesis of N,S-CQDs. The fabricated N,S-CQDs have exhibited an average size of 3.5 nm and with a QY of 20 %. The stability of N,S-CQDs were investigated and optimized sensing conditions to maximize the sensing ability. The calibration curves are linear in the concentration ranges of TT 0–10 μM in PL analysis and 5–35 nM in DPV analysis. The sensor showed excellent analytical performance with a LOD of 80 nM in PL analysis and 2 nM in DPV analysis. Finally, the developed sensor was effectively employed to detect TT concentrations in soft drinks and candy, with excellent recovery and a satisfactory RSD value. Additionally, the N,S-CQDs were successfully applied to develop fluorescent ink for writing, drawing, and thumb impression applications. Compared to the traditional powder dusting technique, the N,S-CQD based wet method of fingerprint imaging is inexpensive, simple, environmentally, and user-friendly.

Bony K. John: Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing – original draft. Sneha Mathew: Software, Methodology, Data curation. Neenamol John: Visualization, Data curation. Jincy Mathew: Visualization, Validation. Beena Mathew: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors were grateful for the financial assistance from UGC India for the Senior Research Fellowship (Grant number BININ01764209).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2022.114386.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
